title: Pick the Right Overload
prompt_md:
## Goal
Given three overloaded functions named `categorize`, write a `main` that reads two values and calls the correct overloads so the output matches exactly.

## Input
Line 1: an integer N.
Line 2: a floating-point number D.

## Output
Line 1: `whole: <N>` (from `categorize(int)`)
Line 2: `fractional: <D>` (from `categorize(double)`, default precision)

## Requirements
1. Define `categorize(int x)` that prints `whole: ` followed by x.
2. Define `categorize(double x)` that prints `fractional: ` followed by x.
3. `main` reads one `int` and one `double` from stdin and calls `categorize` on each.
4. The call to `categorize` on the integer must invoke the `int` overload (not `double`).
5. No extra blank lines in output.

title: Overload Resolution Bug — Fix the Wrong Overload
prompt_md:
## Goal
The program below is supposed to print `int` when given an integer argument and `double` when given a double argument. It contains one logic bug: the wrong overload is selected for one of the calls. Find and fix it.

## Input
Line 1: an integer N (read into an `int` variable).
Line 2: a floating-point number D (read into a `double` variable).

## Output
Line 1: `int: <N>`
Line 2: `double: <D>` (default precision)

## Requirements
1. `label(int x)` prints `int: ` followed by x.
2. `label(double x)` prints `double: ` followed by x.
3. The call with the integer variable must invoke `label(int)`, not `label(double)`.
4. The bug is a logic bug (wrong explicit cast directing to the wrong overload). The starter compiles and runs but prints `double: ` on the first line instead of `int: `.
